title: The Encoder-Decoder Pipeline
concept: The encoder-decoder pipeline downsamples images to extract high-level semantic meaning, then upsamples them to restore spatial resolution.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a major advantage of FCNs over classification networks?

options.text: They can accept input images of any size and preserve spatial layout
They do not use backpropagation during training
They are strictly limited to binary segmentation

exercises.explain: Correct! FCNs are size-agnostic and preserve spatial information.

cards.title: The shrink and stretch
cards.body: When convolutions and pooling layers process an image, they shrink its width and height to extract abstract concepts (like 'car hood' or 'wheel'). But for segmentation, we need to output a mask that is the exact same size as the input. How do we get our spatial resolution back?

cards.title: Encoder-Decoder
cards.body: The Encoder-Decoder design is the standard layout for segmentation. The Encoder downsamples the image to capture context ('what' is in the image). The Decoder upsamples the feature maps to restore precise spatial resolution ('where' it is).

cards.title: Compressing and unpacking
cards.body: Think of the encoder as summarizing a book into bullet points (context). The decoder is like taking those bullet points and writing a detailed scene description (resolution) back onto the canvas.

cards.title: Resolution changes
cards.body: An input image of 256x256 is compressed by the encoder down to 8x8 (capturing high-level features). The decoder then takes this 8x8 representation and upsamples it step-by-step back to 256x256.

cards.title: Not just zooming in
cards.body: You might think the decoder is just a simple zoom tool. It's not! Simple zooming (interpolation) makes things blurry. The decoder actually learns how to rebuild sharp details using trained convolutional filters.

cards.title: Encoder-Decoder Flow
cards.body: The down-and-up shape of segmentation networks:

exercises.prompt: What is the primary role of the decoder in an encoder-decoder network?

options.text: To restore spatial resolution and localize features
To classify the overall scene category
To downsample the input to save memory
To flatten the features into a single class vector

exercises.explain: Correct! The decoder upsamples the feature maps to the original image size.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The encoder is responsible for understanding 'what' is in the image.
exercises.explain: True. The encoder extracts abstract, high-level features that identify objects.

exercises.sentence: While the encoder downsamples the image, the decoder ___ it back to the original size.
exercises.explain: Upsampling is the process of increasing spatial resolution.

exercises.prompt: What problem does the encoder-decoder structure solve in image segmentation?

options.text: The loss of spatial resolution caused by convolutions and pooling

exercises.explain: It restores the spatial dimensions lost during downsampling.

exercises.prompt: Why can't we just use the encoder's deep features directly to create a high-resolution segmentation mask?

options.text: The deep features in the encoder have very low spatial resolution (e.g. 8x8), which makes it impossible to draw sharp, detailed boundaries.
The encoder features do not contain any color information.
Encoder features are stored in a format that cannot be read by GPUs.
The encoder only outputs text labels, not feature maps.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Downsampling destroys high-resolution boundaries, which is why the decoder is essential to upsample them back. Next: how does the decoder learn to upsample?
